title: Superconductivity and electronic structure evolution in the enforced semimetal Fe-doped ZrTe$_2$
authors: L. M. Ishikura, C. F. Schuch, A. Faé Rabello, F. F. Nogueira, L. E. Corrêa, L. R. de Faria, J. Larrea Jiménez, L. T. F. Eleno, A. J. S. Machado
abstract: ZrTe$_2$ is an outstanding layered semimetal due to the topologically nontrivial electronic structure. In this work, we present an investigation of the electronic evolution of ZrTe$_2$ in the presence of Fe intercalation, namely Fe$_{x}$ZrTe$_2$ ($x= 0 - 0.25$), scrutinized by both experimental measurements and \textit{ab} initio calculations. While the first reveals a superconducting state with a maximum critical temperature $T_c = 2.74$ K ($x=$ 0.03), the latter indicates that the topological features of the pristine ZrTe$_2$ is sensitive to the distance between Te atoms and Zr layers. Also, the intercalation of Fe does not modify the non-trivial electronic band structure unlike the band crossings are now shifted slightly below $E_{F}$. In particular, a van Hove singularity near the Fermi level for a Fe content of $x=0.125$ is observed in the density of states, indicating that the superconducting order may be associated with features of the unfolded band structure and the concomitant enhancement of the density of states at $E_F$. Finally, our results reveal that the new compound with inclusion of Fe intercalation preserves the enforced semimetal classification.
summary: This paper demonstrates that intercalating iron into the semimetal ZrTe2 can trigger superconductivity by creating a van Hove singularity near the Fermi level. It also shows that this doping process suppresses the competing charge density wave state while maintaining the material's topological characteristics.
main_problem: The study investigates how Fe intercalation affects the electronic structure, superconductivity, and charge density wave (CDW) stability in the topological semimetal ZrTe2.
main_result: Fe doping induces superconductivity (Tc = 2.74 K) associated with a van Hove singularity near the Fermi level and destabilizes the CDW order, while preserving the enforced semimetal topology.
method: The researchers combined experimental transport measurements (resistivity, magnetoresistance) and X-ray diffraction with ab initio DFT calculations using the Quantum ESPRESSO package and band unfolding techniques.
model_or_system: The physical system is Fe-intercalated ZrTe2 (Fe_xZrTe2), a layered transition metal dichalcogen_2 where Fe atoms occupy octahedral sites in the van der Waals gap.
key_observables: Critical temperature (Tc), CDW transition temperature (T_CDW), upper critical field (Hc2), density of states (DOS), and lattice parameters.
important_parameters: Fe concentration (x = 0 to 0.25), Te-z coordinate, and connectivity value (phi).
main_assumptions: DFT calculations used the PBE-GGA functional and neglected spin-orbit coupling in certain structural relaxation steps; supercells were used to model disordered doping.
figures_summary: Figure 5 shows band structures with and without SOC; Figure 6 displays unfolded band structures for various doped supercells; Figure 7 presents projected density of states; Figure 8 compares different lattice configurations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the material system, details the experimental synthesis and characterization, presents the computational methodology, discusses the evolution of structural and electronic properties, and concludes with the interplay between superconductivity and CDW.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Crystal structure of Fe-doped ZrTe2 in the 2 × 2 × 2 supercell employed in DFT calculations, repre- senting the octahedral intercalation site. (b) 1st Brillouin Zone of SG 164 with highlighted path (red) used to plot the dispersion curves, and extra path H−K (blue) where the other band crossing occurs.

FIG. 2: (a) θ−2θ XRD diffractogram for FexZrTe2 single crystals (x = 0.03, 0.05, 0.06) revealing only (00ℓ) reflections. The insets show the (002) peak shift between different Fe concentrations and pictures of single crystals grown via ICVT, with dimensions up to ∼10.0 × 10.0 × 0.2 mm3; (b) Rocking curve centered at the (004) peak of a Fe0.03ZrTe2 single crystal, with a FWHM = 0.065°; (c) Experimental and DFT calculated lattice parameters c and a as a function of x in FexZrTe2. Experimental c data was obtained by fitting the (00ℓ) reflections from Fig. 2(a).

FIG. 3: (a) Electric resistivity ρ/ρ5.0 K vs. T for FexZrTe2 single crystals (x = 0.03, 0.05, 0.06). The inset shows the low temperature ρ/ρ5.0 K vs. T with Tc = 2.74 K for x = 0.03. Red downward arrow shows the charge density wave transition (TCDW ). (b) Low temperature magnetorresistivity ρ vs. T for Fe0.03ZrTe2 single crystal under magnetic fields up to 0.2 T. (c) Upper critical field Hc2 vs. T fitted to the WHH single band model (dashed line) and two-band model (solid line) for Fe0.03ZrTe2.

FIG. 4: The independent sheets of the Fermi surface of (a) pristine ZrTe2 and (b) intercalated FeZrTe2. The color map indicates the Fermi velocity magnitude in nat- ural units (1 Ry.au ≈2.187 × 106 m/s).

FIG. 5: Band structure of stoichiometric FeZrTe2. In the first (second) line SOC was not considered (included). (a)-(b) A complete plot of the eletronic dispersion along high-symmetry points of the BZ. (c)-(f) Plots of selected paths where the band crossing occurs, with different colors assigned to each band and their respective irreducible representations. Orange and blue points represent +/−inversion eigenvalues, and green +/−symbols represent the {3± 001|0} eigenvalues.
